entries.title: AGI Timelines and Government Preparedness
entries.author: Klein, Ezra
entries.hook: A White House AI adviser confirms the secret government and lab consensus: AGI in 2-3 years, and nobody has a plan.
entries.why_it_matters: Directly addresses the Prologue's governance crisis: 'planning the coronation without drafting a handbook for the peasants.'
entries.summary: Klein explores the two-to-three-year AGI timeline confirmed by multiple sources inside AI labs and government, emphasizing radical societal unpreparedness. The conversation frames AGI arrival during Trump's second term as a governance crisis without precedent.

entries.title: Contrarian View on AGI Timelines
entries.author: Metz, Cade
entries.hook: Silicon Valley's AGI hype might be overblown — and we might need totally new approaches before AI actually gets smarter than us.
entries.why_it_matters: A necessary counterpoint to the Prologue's confident timeline, questioning whether the predicted shift has measurable evidence.
entries.summary: Metz challenges Silicon Valley's optimism about near-term AGI, arguing that current approaches will require fundamental new paradigms before reaching human-level intelligence. The piece features commentary from AI researchers skeptical of accelerated timelines.
entries.bibtex:
@article{metz2025agi,
  author = {Metz, Cade},
  title = {Why We're Unlikely to Get Artificial General Intelligence Anytime Soon},
  journal = {The New York Times},
  year = {2025},
  month = may
}

entries.title: US-China AI Cooperation and High-Tech Feudalism
entries.author: Friedman, Thomas L.
entries.hook: We're heading toward high-tech feudalism unless the US and China can somehow cooperate on AI instead of racing each other to the finish line.
entries.why_it_matters: Frames geopolitical AI competition as the central threat the Prologue warns about — a race nobody can stop.
entries.summary: Friedman argues AGI will substantially emerge within four years and warns of "high-tech feudalism" if geopolitical competition dominates over cooperation. He frames AI safety as the nuclear arms control equivalent of our era.
entries.bibtex:
@article{friedman2024ai,
  author = {Friedman, Thomas L.},
  title = {U.S. and China Must Cooperate on AI},
  journal = {The New York Times},
  year = {2024},
  month = oct
}

entries.title: NYT vs. OpenAI Copyright Lawsuit
entries.author: The New York Times
entries.hook: The New York Times sued OpenAI and Microsoft for stealing their articles to train AI models — raising fundamental questions about who owns AI's brain.
entries.why_it_matters: Exemplifies the power asymmetry the Prologue describes: how AI companies extract value from human knowledge with minimal compensation or consent.
entries.summary: The Times pursued landmark litigation against OpenAI and Microsoft for unauthorized use of copyrighted articles in AI training data. The case raises foundational questions about intellectual property, compensation, and data ownership in the generative AI era.

entries.title: The Economics of Superintelligence
entries.author: The Economist
entries.hook: Superintelligent AI could turbocharge the economy by 20-30% annually — but all that wealth will concentrate at the top unless we actively redistribute it.
entries.why_it_matters: The Prologue's core economic argument: abundance without distribution is just inequality on steroids.
entries.summary: A special three-article package examining how superintelligent AI could drive explosive economic growth of 20–30% annual GDP, while wealth accumulates at unprecedented speed if technological advances outpace human input. The analysis raises the specter of radical inequality even amid abundance.

entries.title: Large Creative AI Models and Labor Markets
entries.author: The Economist
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: ChatGPT and friends are coming for the creative jobs we thought AI would never touch — writers, analysts, designers, everyone smart enough to be valuable.
entries.why_it_matters: Documents the early shock of recognizing that knowledge work is newly vulnerable, not protected by credentials or talent.
entries.summary: Early analysis of how generative AI models like ChatGPT will disrupt creative work and reshape labor markets, affecting both independent and salaried workers in knowledge-intensive fields. The piece identifies creative and analytical roles — long considered automation-proof — as newly vulnerable.

entries.title: Will AI Investments Pay Off?
entries.author: The Economist
entries.hook: Tech companies have bet trillions on AI paying off soon, but we might hit an energy wall or data shortage before it actually works — or it might not.
entries.why_it_matters: Questions whether the Prologue's confident timeline has economic constraints that could delay or derail it entirely.
entries.summary: The Economist examines whether massive AI investments will deliver returns or burst, highlighting energy demands, data shortages, and high training costs as key hurdles. The piece notes that "agentic" AI systems — capable of autonomous end-to-end task completion — are the next frontier.
entries.bibtex:
@article{economist2024gamble,
  author = {{The Economist}},
  title = {History's Biggest Business Gamble: Will AI Start Delivering in 2025?},
  journal = {The Economist},
  year = {2024},
  month = nov
}

entries.title: AI's Transformation of Society
entries.author: Korinek, Anton
questions: What happens to meaning and purpose?
entries.hook: AI's job displacement might be fundamentally different from past tech revolutions — this time, there's no guarantee displaced workers transition to new, better jobs.
entries.why_it_matters: Economist Korinek breaks the historical-precedent narrative: technological unemployment is not inevitable, it's a political choice.
entries.summary: Economist Anton Korinek warns that AI's labor displacement and wealth concentration effects may be structurally different from previous technological revolutions. His analysis, prominently featured in The Economist, argues that without deliberate redistribution, AI could hollow out the middle class entirely.

entries.title: A Better Way to Think About AI
entries.author: Autor, David and Manyika, James
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: The best way to think about AI isn't automation — it's augmentation. Humans and AI working together beat either alone, if we can get the relationship right.
entries.why_it_matters: The Prologue's 'orchestrator' thesis in action: the real future isn't humans replaced by AI, it's humans who can't use AI getting left behind.
entries.summary: Autor and Manyika distinguish between automation tools (which replace) and collaboration tools (which augment), arguing AI should enhance human capability rather than substitute for workers. They cite research showing human-AI teams outperforming both solo professionals and AI alone — the orchestrator model the Prologue anticipates.
entries.bibtex:
@article{autor2025atlantic,
  author = {Autor, David and Manyika, James},
  title = {A Better Way to Think About AI},
  journal = {The Atlantic},
  year = {2025},
  month = aug
}

entries.title: Get a Real Friend
entries.author: Beres, Damon
questions: What happens to love and connection?
What happens to meaning and purpose?
entries.hook: AI chatbots make comforting fake friends, but real intimacy requires effort and vulnerability — something algorithms can never provide.
entries.why_it_matters: The Prologue's warning about authenticity: we're substituting algorithmic comfort for real connection in an era of unprecedented loneliness.
entries.summary: Beres critiques the false promise of AI companionship, arguing that genuine intimacy requires effort and vulnerability that AI cannot provide. The piece warns against substituting algorithmic comfort for authentic human connection in an era of widespread loneliness.
entries.bibtex:
@article{beres2025friend,
  author = {Beres, Damon},
  title = {Get a Real Friend},
  journal = {The Atlantic},
  year = {2025},
  month = dec
}

entries.title: The College Essay Is Dead
entries.author: Marche, Stephen
questions: What happens to meaning and purpose?
entries.hook: AI can write college essays indistinguishable from student work, which means the whole assessment system just broke and nobody has a plan to fix it.
entries.why_it_matters: The Prologue's education crisis: if AI makes traditional achievement meaningless, what do we measure instead?
entries.summary: Marche argues that AI systems like GPT-3 render the traditional college essay obsolete by generating competent prose indistinguishable from student work. The piece challenges educators to rethink assessment entirely — a harbinger of the broader meaning crisis the Prologue describes.
entries.bibtex:
@article{marche2022essay,
  author = {Marche, Stephen},
  title = {The College Essay Is Dead: Nobody Is Prepared for How AI Will Transform Academia},
  journal = {The Atlantic},
  year = {2022},
  month = dec
}

entries.title: The Doomsday Scenario for AI and Jobs
entries.author: Thompson, Derek
entries.hook: Experts predict catastrophic job losses, but the economy isn't showing it yet — and that gap between prediction and data is where the real question lives.
entries.why_it_matters: The Prologue's timing question: is AI the fastest transition we've ever made, or are we misreading the signals?
entries.summary: Thompson examines three scenarios for AI's labor market impact and notes the troubling disconnect between dire expert predictions and present economic data. He argues that how we "play" with AI today will shape future work in ways current models cannot predict.

entries.title: Atlantic Festival 2025: The Future of AI and Humanity
entries.author: The Atlantic
questions: What happens to meaning and purpose?
entries.hook: What does human flourishing look like if we intentionally design technology to expand imagination instead of just maximizing efficiency?
entries.why_it_matters: The Prologue's core design question: can we build AI systems that increase agency rather than erode it?
entries.summary: This festival panel explored human-AI interaction design and how technology can be intentionally shaped to enhance human flourishing rather than erode it. The discussion centered on building AI systems that expand imagination and agency instead of maximizing efficiency.

entries.title: AI Will Test Who We Are as a Species
entries.author: The Guardian
questions: What happens to meaning and purpose?
entries.hook: Anthropic's CEO warns we're about to be handed almost unimaginable power — and it's not clear our institutions are mature enough to handle it.
entries.why_it_matters: Directly echoes the Prologue's central question: the problem isn't AI control, it's human governance.
entries.summary: Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei warns that humanity is entering a critical rite of passage — about to be handed almost unimaginable power — and it is deeply unclear whether our institutions possess the maturity to wield it. The framing mirrors the Prologue's central question: not "can we control AI?" but "can we govern ourselves?"

entries.title: Demis Hassabis: AI Will Be 10x Bigger Than the Industrial Revolution
entries.author: The Guardian
questions: What does AI cost the planet?
entries.hook: The CEO of Google DeepMind says AI will be 10 times bigger than the Industrial Revolution and happen 10 times faster — with unpredictable disruption at every level.
entries.why_it_matters: The Prologue's speed claim: this isn't gradual technological change, it's civilizational shock.
entries.summary: Hassabis warns that generative AI will bring about change "10 times bigger than the Industrial Revolution, and maybe 10 times faster." The wide-ranging conversation covers workplace disruption, scientific opportunity, and the environmental cost of training large models.

entries.title: The Guardian's Reworked Initiative
entries.author: The Guardian
entries.hook: A year-long investigation into how millions of real workers are already using (or managed by) AI — not Silicon Valley hype, but actual human disruption.
entries.why_it_matters: The Prologue's worker-centered framing: AI's impact is only visible from the perspective of those losing control.
entries.summary: The Guardian launched a comprehensive year-long reporting project placing workers — not tech executives — at the center of the AI narrative. The series documents how millions are already working alongside or being managed by AI, interrogating the simplistic "AI destroys jobs vs. AI is harmless" binary.

entries.title: Jaron Lanier on AI and Meaning
entries.author: The Guardian
questions: What happens to meaning and purpose?
Am I still the real me?
entries.hook: Tech pioneer Jaron Lanier warns the real AI risk isn't rogue superintelligence — it's the erosion of meaning-making itself.
entries.why_it_matters: The Prologue's deepest concern: AI doesn't need to rebel, it just needs to make human discourse more confusing.
entries.summary: Lanier argues the real AI risk is not rogue superintelligence but the erosion of human meaning-making — if AI makes communication more confusing and disconnected, society could descend into collective disorientation. His warning echoes the Prologue's thesis that we mastered prompts but didn't master our days.

entries.title: Climate Data Deletion Under Trump Administration
entries.author: The Guardian
questions: What does AI cost the planet?
entries.hook: The Trump administration systematically deleted climate datasets and science, and journalists had to reconstruct what was deliberately erased.
entries.why_it_matters: The Prologue's SusanHub parallel: how networks can preserve what power wants to erase.
entries.summary: The Guardian tracked and compiled climate datasets systematically deleted by the Trump administration, including the removal of National Climate Assessments and shutdown of key NOAA data products. The reporting documents a "denial by erasure" strategy targeting publicly funded climate science — the same threat the Prologue's SusanHub network mobilized against.

entries.title: AI Job Displacement on Wall Street
entries.author: Wall Street Journal
entries.hook: Financial firms are cutting 200,000 jobs as AI automates trading, analysis, and compliance work that used to require armies of credential-holding professionals.
entries.why_it_matters: The Prologue's first shock wave: credentialed knowledge work is newly vulnerable, not protected by intelligence or education.
entries.summary: Financial services firms are projected to cut 200,000 positions as AI automates trading, analysis, and compliance functions that previously required large teams of knowledge workers. The cuts represent one of the first large-scale sector disruptions where "good enough" AI replaces credentialed professionals — the dynamic the Prologue predicts.

entries.title: The AI Skill Premium
entries.author: Forbes
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: Workers who can orchestrate AI command 56% higher wages than peers doing equivalent work — orchestration is the new scarce skill.
entries.why_it_matters: The Prologue's central claim: intelligence is no longer scarce, but the ability to direct intelligence is.
entries.summary: Workers with AI orchestration and prompt engineering skills command a 56% wage premium over peers in equivalent roles, while the traditional skill premium for knowledge work is flattening. This bifurcation illustrates the Prologue's claim that orchestration — not intelligence — is the new scarce resource.

entries.title: Emergent Abilities of Large Language Models
entries.author: Wei, Jason et al.
entries.hook: Skills like arithmetic and translation appear unpredictably in large language models beyond certain size thresholds — capabilities that emerge uninvited and unexplained.
entries.why_it_matters: The academic foundation for the Prologue's claim: AI capabilities arrive 'uninvited, unexplained' once models cross a threshold.
entries.summary: Defines "emergent abilities" as capabilities that arise unpredictably in large language models but are absent in smaller ones, documenting how skills like arithmetic, translation, and reasoning appear only beyond certain scale thresholds. This is the academic backbone for the Prologue's claim that AI capabilities appeared "uninvited, unexplained" once models crossed a certain size.

entries.title: Are Emergent Abilities a Mirage?
entries.author: Schaeffer, Rylan et al.
entries.hook: The dramatic shift in AI capabilities might be an illusion created by how we measure progress, not actual discontinuity in what models do.
entries.why_it_matters: A necessary check on the Prologue's dramatic framing: maybe the revolution is more gradual than we think.
entries.summary: Challenges the emergence hypothesis by demonstrating that apparent emergent abilities result from metric choices rather than fundamental changes in model behavior. The paper suggests the "phase change" narrative may be partly an artifact of measurement — a useful counterpoint to the Prologue's dramatic framing.

entries.title: Attention Is All You Need
entries.author: Vaswani, Ashish et al.
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: The foundational paper that made modern AI possible: the Transformer architecture that powers every GPT, Claude, and Gemini you've used.
entries.why_it_matters: The technical foundation for the Prologue's entire premise: this architecture made the AI revolution possible.
entries.summary: Introduces the Transformer architecture — the "T" in GPT — based purely on attention mechanisms, enabling models to process entire sequences in parallel rather than one token at a time. Cited over 173,000 times, this paper is the technical foundation for the generative AI revolution the Prologue describes.

entries.title: Artificial Intelligence, Values, and Alignment
entries.author: Gabriel, Iason
entries.hook: Making AI obey instructions (technical alignment) is totally different from making sure those instructions encode values we actually want (normative alignment).
entries.why_it_matters: The Prologue's governance question: control and alignment aren't the same thing.
entries.summary: Distinguishes between technical alignment (how to make systems obey) and normative alignment (what values should be encoded), arguing that aligning with instructions, intentions, preferences, and values are fundamentally different challenges. Directly relevant to the Prologue's question: "Where's the playbook for human alignment?"
entries.bibtex:
@article{gabriel2020alignment,
  author = {Gabriel, Iason},
  title = {Artificial Intelligence, Values, and Alignment},
  journal = {Minds and Machines},
  year = {2020},
  volume = {30},
  number = {3},
  pages = {411--437},
  doi = {10.1007/s11023-020-09539-2},
  url = {https://arxiv.org/abs/2001.09768}
}

entries.title: Ghost Work: The Hidden Human Labor Behind AI
entries.author: Gray, Mary L. and Suri, Siddharth
entries.hook: Behind every 'automated' AI system is an invisible global workforce of data labelers making $2 an hour to train models worth billions.
entries.why_it_matters: The Prologue's exact example: ImageNet's 50,000 workers labeling 14 million images at two cents each.
entries.summary: Reveals the invisible global workforce — Mechanical Turk workers, content moderators, data labelers — earning around $2/hour to power AI systems that appear automated. Directly supports the Prologue's account of ImageNet's 50,000 workers labeling 14 million images at two cents each.

entries.title: Power and Progress
entries.author: Acemoglu, Daron and Johnson, Simon
entries.hook: Automation doesn't automatically create prosperity — whether AI makes us richer or hollowed-out depends entirely on political choices we make now.
entries.why_it_matters: The Prologue's core argument: we need 'a new story about what our lives are for,' which requires political action, not just technology.
entries.summary: Historical and economic analysis arguing that automation does not automatically increase wages or prosperity — outcomes depend on political choices to invest in worker-complementary rather than worker-replacing technologies. A key academic counterweight to techno-optimism, directly relevant to the Prologue's argument that preparation requires "a new story about what our lives are for."

entries.title: The Algorithmic Self: AI and Human Identity
entries.author: Frontiers in Psychology Contributors
questions: What happens to meaning and purpose?
Am I still the real me?
entries.hook: As algorithms mediate more of our lives, the sense of self itself becomes algorithmic — but human identity is embodied, emotional, and culturally rooted in ways AI cannot be.
entries.why_it_matters: The Prologue's psychological anchor: humans remain fundamentally different from AI, even as AI shapes our lives.
entries.summary: Explores how algorithmic mediation reshapes identity construction, raising questions about authenticity and autonomy as AI increasingly shapes self-awareness and introspection. Argues that human cognition remains embodied, emotionally grounded, and culturally embedded despite AI capabilities — a psychological anchor for second place.

entries.title: Neanderthal Extinction: Demography, Not Cognition
entries.author: Bocquet-Appel, Jean-Pierre and Degioanni, Anna
questions: What happens to meaning and purpose?
entries.hook: Neanderthals didn't lose to Homo sapiens because we were smarter — we won because we had more people and better luck with reproduction.
entries.why_it_matters: The Prologue's core metaphor: small advantages compound catastrophically over time, even without dramatic intellectual gaps.
entries.summary: Archaeological consensus now holds that Neanderthal extinction resulted primarily from demographic factors — smaller populations, lower birth rates — rather than cognitive inferiority to Homo sapiens. This complicates the Prologue's "slight cognitive edge" narrative while reinforcing the broader point: even small advantages compound catastrophically over time.

entries.title: Life 3.0: Being Human in the Age of AI
entries.author: Tegmark, Max
questions: What happens to meaning and purpose?
entries.hook: What does it mean to be human if there's a superintelligent species in the room? One of the first comprehensive explorations of second place.
entries.why_it_matters: The Prologue's direct predecessor: Life 3.0 frames the 'second species' question that this book takes as its starting point.
entries.summary: Comprehensive exploration of how AGI might reshape civilization, examining implications for employment, inequality, warfare, meaning, and what it means to be human alongside superintelligent AI. One of the first books to frame the "second species" question the Prologue now takes as its starting point.

entries.title: Minds, Brains, and Programs
entries.author: Searle, John R.
entries.hook: The Chinese Room thought experiment: a machine can manipulate symbols perfectly without understanding a single thing — fluency isn't comprehension.
entries.why_it_matters: The Prologue's key insight: AI narrates without reasoning, and 'fluency takes effort' is a heuristic we misapply to machines.
entries.summary: Foundational argument that symbol manipulation alone cannot constitute genuine understanding or consciousness — a machine processing Chinese characters according to rules does not thereby "understand" Chinese. Directly relevant to the Prologue's observation that AI narrates without reasoning and that "fluency takes effort" is a heuristic we misapply to machines.

entries.title: AI for Conservation: A Horizon Scan
entries.author: Current Biology Contributors
questions: What does AI cost the planet?
entries.hook: AI can revolutionize conservation by identifying species and analyzing habitat — but also risks colonizing indigenous ecological knowledge and centralizing control.
entries.why_it_matters: The Prologue's MonoSOS project: AI as a nervous system for planetary monitoring, with risks of power consolidation.
entries.summary: Surveys AI applications in biodiversity monitoring, species identification, habitat analysis, and conservation planning — the academic context for the Prologue's MonoSOS monkey bridge project. Also highlights risks of AI colonialism and the marginalization of indigenous ecological knowledge.

entries.title: Dissecting Racial Bias in Healthcare Algorithms
entries.author: Obermeyer, Ziad et al.
entries.hook: A widely used healthcare algorithm systematically denied treatment to Black patients because it optimized for cost prediction, not illness severity.
entries.why_it_matters: The Prologue's warning: hidden structural harm happens when we master tools without mastering consequences.
entries.summary: Demonstrates that a widely used healthcare algorithm systematically discriminated against Black patients by predicting healthcare costs rather than illness severity. A powerful example of AI systems encoding existing biases — the kind of hidden structural harm the Prologue warns about when it says we mastered tools without mastering consequences.

entries.title: Ezra Klein: The Government Knows AGI Is Coming
entries.author: Klein, Ezra
entries.hook: Ben Buchanan, a former White House AI adviser, reveals that multiple AI labs and government sources confirm AGI in years, with unprecedented labor market shock.
entries.why_it_matters: The institutional unpreparedness the Prologue warns about: we see the timeline clearly, but have no governance plan.
entries.summary: Buchanan reveals that multiple AI labs and government sources confirm AGI arrival within years, with labor market disruption concentrated in cognitively demanding roles — coding, marketing, research, analysis. The conversation frames institutional unpreparedness as the central political crisis of the decade.

entries.title: Sam Harris: Can We Survive AI?
entries.author: Harris, Sam
questions: What happens to meaning and purpose?
entries.hook: AI alignment researcher Eliezer Yudkowsky presents the strongest case that superintelligence might be uncontrollable, even if we try our hardest.
entries.why_it_matters: The Prologue's existential counterpoint: not doomsday, but a rigorous argument for why optimism requires active work.
entries.summary: Yudkowsky and Soares discuss their book "If Anyone Builds It, Everyone Dies," covering alignment impossibility arguments and superintelligence timelines. The episode asks why many tech leaders remain skeptical of existential risk despite mathematical arguments for concern — the strongest "doomsday" counterpoint to the Prologue's measured optimism.

entries.title: Lex Fridman: Roman Yampolskiy on Dangers of Superintelligent AI
entries.author: Fridman, Lex
questions: What happens to meaning and purpose?
entries.hook: AI risks aren't just existential (machines rebel) or physical (suffering) — they're meaning-loss: a world where human purpose becomes irrelevant.
entries.why_it_matters: The Prologue's core concern, formalized as I-risk: loss of identity and meaning in second place.
entries.summary: Yampolskiy categorizes AI risks as existential (X-risk), suffering (S-risk), and meaning-loss (I-risk), arguing AGI control is fundamentally impossible once systems surpass human cognition. The "I-risk" category — loss of meaning — maps directly onto the Prologue's core concern about human purpose in second place.

entries.title: Lex Fridman: Sam Altman on GPT-5, Power, and AGI
entries.author: Fridman, Lex
entries.hook: OpenAI's CEO discusses the power dynamics of building the most powerful technology in history while trying to maintain human oversight.
entries.why_it_matters: The Prologue's governance paradox: can we control what we don't fully understand?
entries.summary: Altman discusses OpenAI's trajectory toward AGI, covering GPT-5 capabilities, governance challenges, and the power dynamics of AI leadership at civilizational scale. The interview captures the tension between building the most powerful technology in history and maintaining meaningful human oversight.

entries.title: Freakonomics: New Technologies Always Scare Us
entries.author: Dubner, Stephen
entries.hook: Telephone operators and typists adapted to new tech instead of disappearing — but economist David Autor isn't sure AI will follow the same pattern.
entries.why_it_matters: The Prologue's transition question: is AI different from past tech revolutions, or just faster?
entries.summary: Autor examines historical technology adoption patterns, noting that displaced workers (telephone operators, typists) transitioned to new sectors rather than permanent unemployment. The episode asks whether AI follows the same pattern or represents something structurally different — the Prologue's "pretzel-shaped career" question with economic rigor.

entries.title: Freakonomics: What's Impacting American Workers?
entries.author: Dubner, Stephen
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: Jobs aren't solid things that AI destroys — they're bundles of tasks that get rearranged, and AI could rebuild middle-class roles if we design it right.
entries.why_it_matters: The Prologue's task-bundle insight: meaning at work has always migrated; AI is just faster change.
entries.summary: Autor presents research showing jobs are task-bundles where automation redefines rather than eliminates work, with AI potentially rebuilding middle-class roles by augmenting expertise rather than replacing experts. The "task-bundle" framing supports the Prologue's argument that meaning at work has always migrated — from whole shoes to factory shifts to digital abstraction.

entries.title: TED 2025: Will AI Make Humans Useless?
entries.author: Awad, Akram
questions: What happens to meaning and purpose?
entries.hook: As AI replaces jobs, it erodes the identity structures built on professional achievement — so who are we if we can't be useful?
entries.why_it_matters: The Prologue's identity crisis: meaning and dignity separate from economic productivity.
entries.summary: Awad examines three emerging human categories in an AI-displaced workforce, arguing that as jobs disappear, so will the identity structures built on professional achievement. The talk directly addresses the Prologue's warning that "being smart" is no longer the main advantage.

entries.title: TED 2025: Tristan Harris on AI as Humanity's Wisdom Test
entries.author: Harris, Tristan
questions: What happens to meaning and purpose?
entries.hook: We learned to swipe instead of to sleep — and if we deploy AI with social media's playbook, the outcome at AI scale will be catastrophic.
entries.why_it_matters: The Prologue's iPhone analogy: we adopted technology without understanding consequences.
entries.summary: Harris frames AI deployment as humanity's ultimate test of collective wisdom, warning that repeating social media's mistakes — adopting without understanding — would be catastrophic at AI's scale. Directly echoes the Prologue's iPhone analogy: "we learned to swipe, not to sleep."
entries.bibtex:
@misc{harris2025ted,
  author = {Harris, Tristan},
  title = {AI as Humanity's Ultimate Wisdom Test},
  howpublished = {TED2025: Humanity Reimagined},
  year = {2025},
  month = apr
}

entries.title: NPR Consider This: AI and the Environment
entries.author: NPR
questions: What does AI cost the planet?
entries.hook: AI's massive energy footprint accelerates climate change, yet the same technology could solve it — paradox at the heart of AI's future.
entries.why_it_matters: The Prologue's ecological tension: progress comes at environmental cost, and we're not accounting for it.
entries.summary: Investigates the paradox of AI's massive energy consumption and environmental footprint alongside its potential for climate solutions. The episode highlights how the same technology being developed to solve planetary-scale problems is simultaneously accelerating them.

entries.title: All-In Podcast: AI Moratorium Debate
entries.author: Palihapitiya, Chamath et al.
entries.hook: Four VCs debate whether we should pause AI development for safety — and reveal the core tension: you can't stop a race nobody wants to lose.
entries.why_it_matters: The Prologue's governance paradox: collective action problems prevent us from choosing slower, safer paths.
entries.summary: Four prominent venture capitalists debate the merits of an AI development moratorium, weighing economic impact, job displacement, and the tension between competitive advantage and societal readiness. The debate crystallizes the "race nobody can stop" dynamic the book later addresses in Chapter 2.

entries.title: The State of AI: A Vision of the World in 2030
entries.author: Heaven, Will Douglas
questions: What happens to meaning and purpose?
entries.hook: Five years from now, AI might not just be smarter than us—it might be reshaping what we do for work.
entries.why_it_matters: Direct exploration of the prologue's core question: where are we heading in the next five years as AI accelerates?
entries.summary: MIT Technology Review examines AI's trajectory through 2030, exploring how systems will reshape work, society, and human purpose. The conversation emphasizes the acceleration of capabilities and societal readiness gaps.

entries.title: The Widening AI Value Gap
entries.author: BCG Research Team
entries.hook: Only 5% of companies are actually making money from AI. The rest are burning cash.
entries.why_it_matters: Highlights the reality gap: massive investment but minimal value creation, supporting the prologue's theme of frenzied pace without clear direction.
entries.summary: BCG's analysis of 1,250+ firms shows only 5% achieving AI value at scale while 60% report minimal gains. The study reveals agentic AI as the next accelerant, widening the gap between leaders and laggards.

entries.title: Big Tech Set to Spend $650 Billion in 2026 as AI Investments Soar
entries.author: Staff
entries.hook: $650 billion in a single year. That's more than the GDP of most countries, spent on AI infrastructure.
entries.why_it_matters: Embodies the prologue's central image of frenzy—unprecedented capital mobilization for uncertain outcomes.
entries.summary: Four major tech companies (Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, Meta) are collectively spending $650 billion in 2026 on AI data centers and infrastructure, representing a 60-74% increase from 2025. This staggering investment level has few modern parallels.

entries.title: AI Billionaires Boom: How 2025 Minted More Than 50 New Fortunes
entries.author: Staff
entries.hook: 2025 created 50+ new billionaires. One was 22 years old. Welcome to the age of AI wealth concentration.
entries.why_it_matters: Embodies the prologue's concern about power consolidation and the staggering speed of wealth creation in the AI era.
entries.summary: Over 50 new billionaires were created in 2025 through AI investments, with existing tech titans adding $500 billion+ to their wealth. The report includes founders like 22-year-old Surya Midha and highlights unprecedented wealth concentration.

entries.title: The Productivity Paradox Returns: CEOs Admit AI Has No Impact
entries.author: Staff
entries.hook: 90% of CEOs admit AI hasn't improved productivity. Yet companies keep spending billions. Welcome to the Solow Paradox 2.0.
entries.why_it_matters: The prologue's central tension: frantic investment and capability advancement alongside zero demonstrated economic benefit.
entries.summary: A Fortune analysis of nearly 6,000 executives reveals 90% report no AI impact on productivity or employment at their firms. Only 10% quantified impact and 1% showed earnings effects, echoing the 1980s Solow Productivity Paradox.

entries.title: AI for Science 2025: Breakthroughs and Challenges
entries.author: Ledford, Heidi
questions: What does AI cost the planet?
entries.hook: DeepSeek just built a model that matches the best in the world for a fraction of the cost. Science will never be the same.
entries.why_it_matters: Illustrates both the prologue's theme of speed and capability emergence, plus the concerning economic inequality dimension.
entries.summary: Nature reports on AI breakthroughs in 2025 including generative models for biological macromolecules and DeepSeek's surprisingly capable and cost-effective model. PhyE2E demonstrates AI discovering physics formulas directly from data.

entries.title: From Reasoning to Super-Intelligence: A Search-Theoretic Perspective
entries.author: Yao, Yuan
entries.hook: Researchers just showed how to turn reasoning into superintelligence. It's not metaphorical—it's mathematical.
entries.why_it_matters: Directly addresses the prologue's central claim: AI systems are becoming smarter at an accelerating rate through new reasoning paradigms.
entries.summary: This arXiv paper presents a search-theoretic framework for understanding reasoning as depth-first search guided by validators, with implications for scaling intelligence. It demonstrates how reasoning models can efficiently learn from Chain-of-Thought data.

entries.title: How Quickly Will AI Agents Rip Through the Economy?
entries.author: Klein, Ezra
entries.hook: An economist's blunt assessment: AI agents will remake the economy faster than we can even describe it.
entries.why_it_matters: Captures the prologue's anxiety about unstoppable economic disruption without clear plans for transition or retraining.
entries.summary: Ezra Klein hosts a conversation exploring agentic AI's potential to rapidly disrupt labor markets and economic structures. The episode examines how autonomous agents could compress job displacement timelines and challenge existing policy frameworks.

entries.title: How Afraid of the AI Apocalypse Should We Be?
entries.author: Klein, Ezra
entries.hook: Eliezer Yudkowsky: we should be extremely afraid. And not in the sci-fi way—in the mathematical, inevitable-failure way.
entries.why_it_matters: Represents the prologue's undercurrent of existential concern beneath the celebration of progress.
entries.summary: Ezra Klein interviews AI safety pioneer Eliezer Yudkowsky on existential risks from superintelligence. Yudkowsky makes the case for extreme caution, arguing current safety approaches may be fundamentally inadequate.

entries.title: What's Next for AI in 2026
entries.author: Heaven, Will Douglas
entries.hook: 2025 was the hype check. 2026 will be when AI moves from lab to life—fast.
entries.why_it_matters: Directly addresses the prologue's central question of what happens when AI capability meets real-world deployment.
entries.summary: MIT Technology Review examines the transition from AI development cycles to actual deployment and economic impact. Heaven explores how 2026 will test whether theoretical capabilities translate to practical transformation.

entries.title: Future Progress in Artificial Intelligence: A Survey of Expert Opinion
entries.author: Grace, Katja
entries.hook: Expert survey: median estimate puts superintelligence 30-50 years away. And they're probably being optimistic.
entries.why_it_matters: Anchors the prologue's timeline claims in expert consensus about the pace and inevitability of advanced AI development.
entries.summary: This comprehensive survey of AI researchers shows a median estimate of 50% probability for high-level machine intelligence by 2040-2050, with superintelligence following within 30 years. Experts project transformative economic and social impacts.

entries.title: Why AI Governance Gained Ground in 2025
entries.author: Staff
entries.hook: Only 25% of organizations have functional AI governance. Yet it's legally required. Welcome to the compliance apocalypse.
entries.why_it_matters: Illustrates the prologue's governance failure theme: rapid capability advancement while institutions scramble to catch up.
entries.summary: CIO Dive reports that 54% of IT leaders now rank AI governance as a core concern (doubled from 29% in 2024), yet only 25% have fully operational governance frameworks. Board engagement has increased, but execution lags far behind policy.

entries.title: The Adolescence of Technology: What Dario Amodei Gets Right About AI's Next Phase
entries.author: Amodei, Dario
entries.hook: Dario Amodei: AI isn't approaching maturity—it's barely begun its adolescent growth spurt. And adolescence is dangerous.
entries.why_it_matters: Frames the prologue's urgency: we're not nearing a steady state, but entering a period of maximum unpredictability and risk.
entries.summary: Amodei proposes that AI capabilities will reach human-level general intelligence by 2026-2027, entering an 'adolescent' phase characterized by rapid, unpredictable growth and potential harm. He argues current safety measures are inadequate for this transition.

entries.title: Visualizing AI vs. Human Performance in Technical Tasks
entries.author: Visual Capitalist Research Team
entries.hook: Charts showing AI now surpassing humans on standardized benchmarks across math, reasoning, coding, and visual tasks—the moment when 'second place' becomes official.
entries.why_it_matters: Directly illustrates the Prologue's central claim: humans are no longer the smartest species. This provides concrete benchmark comparisons showing where AI exceeds human performance.
entries.summary: A visual analysis comparing AI capabilities to human performance across technical benchmarks. Shows how AI has crossed thresholds in math reasoning, coding tasks, and visual understanding where human experts previously held advantages.

entries.title: Intelligence Evolved at Least Twice in Vertebrate Animals
entries.author: Quanta Magazine Science Writers
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: Birds and mammals independently evolved intelligence—suggesting intelligence itself is not unique but a recurring evolutionary solution. What does this mean for AI as a third path?
entries.why_it_matters: Supports the Prologue's Neanderthal analogy: intelligence is not one-dimensional but a spectrum. Multiple species evolved cognitive sophistication independently; multiple intelligences can coexist.
entries.summary: Recent research reveals that birds and mammals did not inherit neural pathways for intelligence from a common ancestor but evolved them independently. This suggests intelligence as an evolutionary solution emerges across taxa, challenging the notion of human cognitive uniqueness.

entries.title: Responsible Use of AI for Nature Protection and Preservation
entries.author: World Economic Forum Contributors
questions: What does AI cost the planet?
entries.hook: AI systems monitoring rainforests in real-time, protecting biodiversity at scale—but only if humanity chooses to use this nervous system for planetary health rather than extraction.
entries.why_it_matters: Connects to Serpa's MonoSOS and SusanHub networks: AI as the planet's nervous system. Shows concrete applications in conservation (Project Guacamaya, tree canopy mapping) where AI amplifies human sustainability efforts.
entries.summary: Explores how AI companies and tech platforms are leveraging machine learning for nature protection, including satellite monitoring, bioacoustics, and real-time ecosystem tracking. Examples include Google's Tree Canopy tool and Microsoft's conservation partnerships.

entries.title: Global AI Adoption in 2025: A Widening Digital Divide
entries.author: Microsoft AI Economy Institute
entries.hook: While 64% of working-age people in the UAE use AI, only 14.1% do in the Global South. The 'second place' is not just species-level but stratified by nation, wealth, and infrastructure access.
entries.why_it_matters: Directly addresses the Prologue's implicit concern: AI transformation is not happening evenly. Global North adoption runs at double the rate of Global South. US and China control 86% of data center capacity—concentration of power mirrors historical colonial patterns.
entries.summary: Microsoft's comprehensive report on global AI adoption shows 16.3% of world population now uses generative AI, but adoption is deeply uneven. Geographic divides (Global North 24.7% vs Global South 14.1%), demographic gaps (53.6 percentage points by age), and infrastructure concentration (US-China duopoly on data centers) define the emerging AI landscape.

entries.title: 20-Year Study Reveals: Neanderthals Were As Intelligent as Homo sapiens
entries.author: SciTechDaily Research Team
questions: Am I still the real me?
entries.hook: Neanderthals weren't dimmer—they just had different cognitive priorities. Larger eyes and motor control consumed more brain resources. Today's humans aren't inherently smarter, just organized differently for social cognition.
entries.why_it_matters: Anchors the Prologue's inverted Neanderthal analogy. The key insight: intelligence is not monolithic but context-dependent. Neanderthals excelled at survival in harsh climates but couldn't scale social networks like modern humans. What will AI's cognitive 'specialization' be—and will humans find ourselves similarly limited by comparison?
entries.summary: Long-term neurological research compares Neanderthal and modern human brains. While Neanderthals had larger overall brain size, human brains allocated more resources to front-lobe social cognition, enabling larger social networks. Neanderthals demonstrated sophisticated tool use, art creation, and environmental adaptation—intelligence expressed through different priorities.
